A2.title: How volcanoes erupt
A2.body: Have you ever seen a volcano on TV? It looks like a mountain that breathes fire. But why does it happen? Deep under the ground, it is very hot. So hot that rocks can melt. This melted rock is called magma. Magma is lighter than the rock around it, so it slowly rises. When it finds a weak spot, it pushes up and out. Then we call it lava. Sometimes gas inside the magma makes a big boom. That is why some eruptions are very loud and strong. After the eruption, the lava cools down and becomes new rock. Volcanoes are scary, but they also build new land.

B1.title: How volcanoes erupt
B1.body: A volcano may look quiet for years, but inside the Earth, things are always moving. Far below our feet, the rock is so hot that some of it melts into a thick liquid called magma. This magma sits in a kind of underground room, a magma chamber. Because magma is lighter than solid rock, it slowly tries to rise toward the surface. As it rises, gases that were trapped inside it start to escape, just like bubbles in a shaken bottle of soda. If the magma is thin, the gas comes out gently and lava flows quietly down the side of the mountain. But if the magma is thick and sticky, the gas cannot escape easily. Pressure builds up until the volcano explodes, throwing ash, rocks, and lava into the sky. Once the eruption ends, the lava cools and forms new rock. Over thousands of years, these eruptions can build whole islands.

key_phrases.phrase: just like bubbles in a shaken bottle of soda
throwing ash, rocks, and lava into the sky

B2.title: How volcanoes erupt
B2.body: From the outside, a volcano can seem almost peaceful, but the story underneath it is much more dramatic. The Earth is built in layers, and the layer just below the crust is so hot that pockets of rock melt into a thick liquid called magma. This magma collects in a magma chamber, often several kilometers below the surface. Because magma is less dense than the solid rock around it, gravity actually works in its favor and slowly pushes it upward through cracks in the crust. What really drives an eruption, though, is gas. Magma contains dissolved gases like water vapor and carbon dioxide, kept under enormous pressure deep underground. As the magma rises and that pressure drops, the gases expand and form bubbles, just like opening a bottle of fizzy water. If the magma is runny, the gas escapes smoothly and the eruption is gentle, with rivers of lava sliding down the slopes. But when the magma is thick, the bubbles get trapped, pressure builds rapidly, and the volcano explodes violently. These powerful eruptions can send clouds of ash high into the atmosphere and even change global temperatures for a year or two. Scientists watch volcanoes closely, using ground sensors, satellites, and tiny shifts in gas chemistry to predict when an eruption might be on the way. It is not a perfect science, but it has already saved countless lives in places like Indonesia and Iceland. Once everything calms down, the lava cools, hardens, and slowly adds new layers to the volcano. In a sense, every eruption is both a destruction and an act of creation.

key_phrases.phrase: both a destruction and an act of creation

C1.title: How volcanoes erupt
C1.body: Looking at a snow-capped volcano on a calm day, it is hard to imagine that it is essentially a pressure cooker connected to the deep interior of our planet. Yet that is exactly what it is. The Earth's outer shell, the crust, sits on top of a hotter, partially molten layer called the mantle. Where tectonic plates pull apart or collide, conditions allow pockets of mantle rock to melt, producing magma that gathers in chambers a few kilometers below the surface. Magma is buoyant compared to the surrounding rock, so it inches upward through fractures and weak zones, often over hundreds or thousands of years. The decisive ingredient in any eruption, however, is the gas dissolved within that magma. Water, carbon dioxide, and sulfur compounds remain locked in solution as long as the pressure stays high. As the magma climbs and pressure falls, those gases come out of solution and form bubbles, in a process scientists call exsolution. The viscosity of the magma then determines what happens next. Low-viscosity basaltic magma, common in places like Hawaii, allows gas to escape easily, producing the relatively gentle, flowing eruptions that tourists can sometimes watch from a safe distance. High-viscosity rhyolitic or andesitic magma, by contrast, traps gas until pressure overwhelms the surrounding rock, and the result can be a catastrophic explosion that ejects ash, pumice, and pyroclastic flows at terrifying speeds. Modern volcanology relies on a layered defence to anticipate these events. Seismometers detect the tiny earthquakes that swarming magma produces, GPS instruments record the gradual swelling of the ground, and gas spectrometers track changes in the ratio of sulfur dioxide to carbon dioxide that often precede major activity. None of these signals are individually reliable, but together they have allowed volcanologists to issue useful warnings before several recent eruptions, saving thousands of lives. The aftermath is just as significant as the eruption itself. Cooled lava and ash gradually build new land, fertile volcanic soils support some of the world's most productive agriculture, and sulfur aerosols injected into the stratosphere can subtly cool the planet for months. Volcanoes are, in this sense, both architects and disrupters, continually reshaping the surface we walk on.

A2.title: How the printing press changed the world
A2.body: Long ago, books were very rare. People had to copy every book by hand. It took months or even years to make just one book. Because of this, only rich people and priests had books. Most people could not read at all. Then a man named Gutenberg made a new machine in Germany. It was called the printing press. With this machine, one worker could print many pages in a day. Soon, books became cheaper. More people learned to read. New ideas spread very fast from one city to another. Many people say this machine changed the world forever.

B1.title: How the printing press changed the world
B1.body: Before the 15th century, every book in Europe was written by hand. Monks worked for months in quiet rooms, copying page after page. Because the process was so slow, books were extremely expensive, and most ordinary people could not even read. Knowledge was something that stayed inside churches, palaces, and a few rich families. Then, around 1440, a German craftsman called Johannes Gutenberg invented a new kind of machine: the printing press with movable metal letters. Suddenly, the same page could be printed hundreds of times in a single day. Within fifty years, millions of books had been printed all across Europe. Bibles, science books, poems, and political pamphlets traveled from city to city much faster than before. More people learned to read, and ordinary citizens started to discuss ideas that used to belong only to experts. In a way, the printing press was the first information technology, and it set off a wave of change that we can still feel today.

key_phrases.phrase: used to belong only to experts
set off a wave of change

B2.title: How the printing press changed the world
B2.body: It is hard for us today, surrounded by phones and laptops, to picture a world where almost no one could read. Yet that was the reality across most of Europe before the middle of the 15th century. Every single book had to be copied by hand, usually by monks in monasteries, and a single Bible could take more than a year to produce. Books were so rare and costly that they were chained to library shelves, and knowledge was effectively locked away from the general population. Then, around 1440, a goldsmith named Johannes Gutenberg combined several existing technologies in a new way. He cast individual metal letters that could be arranged into words, lines, and pages, and then pressed onto paper using a modified wine press. What made this revolutionary was the speed and the consistency. A single workshop could print in a day what a monk used to copy in months, and every copy was almost identical. By 1500, only sixty years after Gutenberg's invention, an estimated twenty million books were circulating in Europe. This sudden flood of cheap printed material reshaped religion, science, and politics in ways that nobody had predicted. Martin Luther's reform ideas spread across the continent within weeks, scientists could finally read and build on one another's work, and ordinary citizens began to form opinions on public matters. Universities expanded, literacy rates climbed, and even popular entertainment shifted as cheap novels and pamphlets reached wider audiences. Looking back, the printing press is often seen as the first true mass medium, and its effects laid the foundation for the modern world.

key_phrases.phrase: It is hard for us today

C1.title: How the printing press changed the world
C1.body: Few inventions in human history have produced such a sweeping transformation in such a short time as the printing press. To appreciate just how radical the change was, it helps to remember the world it replaced. Throughout the medieval period in Europe, books were luxury objects, painstakingly copied by hand in monastic scriptoria, often illuminated with delicate paintings, and stored under lock and key. Literacy rates among the general population were vanishingly small, and the production of new knowledge was bottlenecked by sheer physical labor. Around 1440, Johannes Gutenberg of Mainz brought together several existing techniques, including movable metal type, oil-based ink, and a screw-driven press adapted from winemaking, and arrived at a system whose productivity dwarfed everything that came before it. The economic implications were profound almost immediately. The cost of producing a book fell by roughly two orders of magnitude over the following decades, and historians estimate that more books were produced in the fifty years after Gutenberg than in the entire previous millennium of European history. Cheap, identical copies meant that ideas could now travel without being distorted by individual scribes, which had enormous consequences for science. Researchers could finally read the same diagrams, refer to the same page numbers, and build cumulatively on shared evidence, a precondition for what we now call the Scientific Revolution. Politically, the press undermined the monopoly that the Church and aristocracy had long held over information. Martin Luther's Ninety-Five Theses circulated across Europe within weeks, sparking the Reformation, and pamphlets eventually became a central tool of every political movement from the English Civil War to the American and French Revolutions. Culturally, the standardization of texts helped consolidate national vernacular languages, gradually displacing Latin as the universal language of learning, which in turn nurtured the sense of shared identity on which modern nation-states would later be built. There were costs as well as benefits. The same printing networks that spread reformist ideas also distributed propaganda, conspiracy theories, and accusations of witchcraft on an unprecedented scale. In retrospect, the printing press did not merely make existing knowledge cheaper to distribute; it changed who was allowed to participate in public conversation, and it set the basic pattern of every later information revolution, from the telegraph to the internet.

key_phrases.phrase: dwarfed everything that came before it
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 그 이전의 모든 것을 무색하게 만들었다
공유된 증거 위에 누적적으로 쌓아 올리다

key_phrases.phrase: set the basic pattern of every later

A2.title: How recursion works
A2.body: Recursion is a special idea in programming. It means a function that calls itself. That sounds strange, but it is useful for some problems. Imagine you have a big box, and inside it there are smaller boxes. To open them all, you open one box, then do the same thing with the box inside. Each step is the same job, just with a smaller box. A recursive function works in the same way. It also needs a stop rule. Without a stop rule, it would never end and the program would crash. Many tasks like counting, searching, and walking through a tree use this idea.

B1.title: How recursion works
B1.body: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem. It can feel a little strange at first, because it seems like the function never finishes. But the trick is that every recursive function has two parts. First, there is a base case, which tells the function when to stop. Second, there is a recursive case, which calls the same function with a smaller input. A classic example is calculating factorials. To find the factorial of five, the function multiplies five by the factorial of four, and the factorial of four multiplies four by the factorial of three, and so on. Each call waits for its smaller call to finish, and the answers travel back up the chain. If you forget the base case, the function will keep calling itself until your computer runs out of memory. Used carefully, recursion can make complex problems much shorter and easier to read.

B2.title: How recursion works
B2.body: Recursion is one of those programming ideas that looks almost magical at first glance, but becomes much clearer once you understand what is happening behind the scenes. At its core, a recursive function is simply a function that solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller version of that same problem. Every well-written recursive function has two essential parts. The base case is the simplest version of the problem, the one the function can answer directly without any further calls. The recursive case breaks the bigger problem into a smaller one and delegates that smaller piece to another call of the same function. Behind the scenes, the computer uses something called the call stack. Every time a function is called, the computer pushes a new frame onto the stack with the local variables and the current position. When the function returns, that frame is popped off, and execution continues where the previous call left off. Recursion is a natural fit for problems with a recursive structure, like traversing trees, navigating folders, or implementing divide-and-conquer algorithms such as merge sort. It also leads to remarkably short code: a binary tree traversal that would take dozens of lines with explicit loops often fits in three or four lines when written recursively. However, if you forget the base case, the stack keeps growing until you hit the dreaded stack overflow error. Many languages also offer iterative alternatives, and for very deep problems an explicit loop with a stack can be more memory-friendly. Still, when the structure of the problem matches, recursion often produces code that is shorter and closer to how we would describe the solution in words.

key_phrases.phrase: looks almost magical at first glance
closer to how we would describe

C1.title: How recursion works
C1.body: Few concepts divide beginners and experienced developers as sharply as recursion. To a newcomer it can feel almost circular, while to an experienced programmer it is often the most natural way to express a certain class of problems. At its heart, a recursive function is one that defines its solution in terms of itself, but always on a strictly smaller input that is closer to a known base case. Two ingredients are non-negotiable. The base case anchors the recursion, providing a directly computable answer for the simplest input, and the recursive case must make measurable progress toward that base case. Skip either ingredient and you have invited an infinite chain of calls that will eventually exhaust your runtime. What happens at the machine level is worth understanding. Every function call allocates a stack frame containing parameters, local variables, and the return address. When a function calls itself, a new frame is layered on top of the old one, so the call stack grows in lockstep with recursion depth. When each call returns, its frame is popped and control resumes precisely where it left off, allowing the partial results to ripple back up the chain. This elegant mechanism comes with a real cost. Deep recursion can blow the stack, and in languages without tail-call optimization, even logically tail-recursive functions consume linear stack space. That is why divide-and-conquer algorithms like quicksort or merge sort, recursive descent parsers, and traversals over tree and graph structures benefit so much from recursion, while problems that are naturally iterative are usually better written as loops. There is also a subtler performance concern. Naive recursive solutions to problems with overlapping subproblems, such as a textbook Fibonacci, can be exponentially slow, which is exactly why techniques like memoisation and dynamic programming were invented. Functional languages take recursion further still, often using it as the primary control structure and relying on compiler optimizations to keep the cost manageable. In the end, mastering recursion is less about memorizing patterns than about learning to see problems through a particular lens: ask what the smallest instance looks like, ask how a larger instance can be reduced to a smaller one, and trust the function to handle the rest.

topics.title_ko: 데이터베이스 인덱스는 어떻게 쿼리를 빠르게 만드는가

A2.title: How database indexes speed up queries
A2.body: Imagine a very long book with no table of contents. If you want to find one word, you have to read every page. That is very slow. Now imagine the same book has a list at the end with all the important words and the page numbers. You can jump straight to the right page. Much faster, right? A database index works in the same way. Without an index, the database checks every single row to find what you want. With an index, it knows exactly where to look. That is why a good index can make a slow query feel almost instant.

B1.title: How database indexes speed up queries
B1.body: When a database has only a few rows, every query is fast. But once a table grows to millions of rows, finding the right one can become surprisingly slow. Without help, the database has to read every row from beginning to end. Engineers call this a full table scan, and it is the main reason your app suddenly feels sluggish. An index is a separate, well-organized data structure that lives next to the table. It usually stores the values of one or more columns together with pointers back to the actual rows. Most databases use a B-tree for this, which means the index is sorted and balanced. When you ask for a user with a specific email, the database walks down the B-tree, skipping huge parts of the data, and lands on the right pointer in just a few steps. Indexes are not free, though. They take up extra disk space, and they have to be updated every time you insert, update, or delete a row. The art of indexing is choosing the few columns where the speed gain is worth the cost.

B2.title: How database indexes speed up queries
B2.body: If you have ever watched a query slow down as your data grew, you have probably already met the main reason for adding indexes. Without any helping structure, a relational database answers a question like 'find the user with this email' by reading the entire users table from top to bottom. That is called a full table scan, and for a small table it is harmless, but for a table with tens of millions of rows it can take seconds, which is an eternity for a user waiting on a screen. An index is essentially a smaller, carefully ordered copy of selected columns, kept in sync with the main table. Most relational databases implement this with a B-tree, a balanced tree structure that lets the engine find any value in roughly log n steps instead of n. When a query arrives, the planner decides whether the index can help, walks down the tree until it lands on the matching key, and then jumps to the actual row using a pointer stored in the leaf. Indexes also accelerate range queries, sorting, and certain join strategies, which is why a single well-chosen index can transform the performance of an entire application. The trade-off, of course, is that indexes are not free. They occupy extra disk space, and they have to be maintained on every insert, update, or delete, which slows write traffic and can amplify lock contention. Good database design is therefore not about adding as many indexes as possible, but about choosing the few that match your real query patterns and removing the ones nobody uses.

key_phrases.phrase: transform the performance of an entire application

C1.title: How database indexes speed up queries
C1.body: Indexing sits at the heart of relational database performance, and yet it is one of the areas where intuition tends to fail engineers most often. The fundamental problem an index solves is straightforward. Without any auxiliary structure, the engine answers a predicate like 'where email equals x' by performing a full table scan, an operation whose cost grows linearly with the size of the table. When your table holds a few thousand rows that is invisible, but once you reach tens or hundreds of millions of rows, the linear scan becomes the dominant cost of nearly every read. An index is, conceptually, a separate sorted data structure that maps indexed column values to pointers, called row identifiers or tuple IDs, that locate the underlying row in the heap. In most relational systems, the structure of choice is the B-tree, which guarantees logarithmic time access by keeping the tree shallow and balanced, while still supporting efficient range scans because the leaves are linked in sorted order. When the query planner sees a candidate index, it estimates the cost of using it against the cost of a sequential scan, taking into account selectivity, the expected number of matching rows, and the additional cost of fetching from the heap. A highly selective predicate on a small table can sometimes still favor a sequential scan, which is why DBAs spend so much time looking at execution plans rather than guessing. Indexes also enable patterns beyond point lookups, including ordered iteration, index-only scans when all required columns are covered, and certain merge and hash join strategies. The cost side of the ledger is equally important. Every index multiplies write amplification, since the engine has to maintain the structure on every insert, update, and delete, and that maintenance can become a serious bottleneck under heavy write traffic, especially when combined with hot keys and concurrent transactions. Indexes also occupy non-trivial storage and can pollute the buffer cache if they are rarely used. Mature teams therefore treat indexes as a deliberately curated set rather than a defensive sprinkle, regularly auditing usage statistics, removing dead indexes, and considering more specialized structures like partial, expression, or BRIN indexes when the access pattern justifies the complexity.

key_phrases.phrase: the cost side of the ledger

topics.title_ko: [IELTS P3] 기술은 사람들의 독서 방식을 어떻게 바꿨는가

A2.title: How has technology changed the way people read books?
A2.body: I think technology has changed reading a lot. In the past, people read paper books only. Now many people read on their phone or a tablet. It is easier to carry. You can have many books in one small device. Also, you can read in bed at night because the screen has light. But some people still love paper books. They like the smell and the feeling. So I would say technology gave us more choice.

B1.title: How has technology changed the way people read books?
B1.body: To be honest, I think technology has changed reading in some pretty big ways. A generation ago, if you wanted to read a book, you basically had to go to a bookshop or a library and bring a paper book home. Now, with a phone or an e-reader, you can buy and start a book in less than a minute, and you can carry hundreds of them in your bag. Audiobooks are another thing. A lot of my friends listen to books while commuting or doing the dishes, which they would never have done before. On the other hand, I do feel that reading on a phone is more distracting, because notifications keep pulling you out of the story. So I would say technology has made reading more flexible, but maybe also a bit shallower for some people.

key_phrases.phrase: pulling you out of the story

B2.title: How has technology changed the way people read books?
B2.body: I would say technology has changed reading in ways that are easy to underestimate. Twenty years ago, reading basically meant a paper book, a magazine, or maybe a newspaper. Now, the same person might read on a phone during their commute, on an e-reader before bed, and listen to an audiobook while cooking dinner. The most obvious change is access. With a few taps, you can buy a book that used to take a trip to a bookshop, and free public libraries now let you borrow e-books without ever leaving home. I think this has genuinely helped people read more in absolute terms, especially busy adults who do not have time to browse shelves. Honestly, though, I think there is a downside too. Reading on a phone means competing with messages, social media, and an endless stream of notifications, and that constant interruption can make it harder to stay with a long, complex book. Some studies suggest people remember less from screen reading than from paper, although the effect is not huge. Overall, I think technology has democratised reading, but it has also reshaped what we mean by reading, often making it more fragmented and more visual at the same time.

key_phrases.phrase: stay with a long, complex book
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 길고 복잡한 책을 끝까지 붙들고 가다

key_phrases.phrase: more visual at the same time

C1.title: How has technology changed the way people read books?
C1.body: I think technology has changed reading in ways that are both more profound and more subtle than most people realise. The most visible shift is obviously the move from paper to screens, and from owning physical objects to streaming content from cloud services. On the surface, that just looks like a change of medium, but I would argue it has quietly reshaped the whole experience. Take access, for example. A generation ago, reading something obscure or in a foreign language could be genuinely difficult, but now almost any book is a few taps away, often in multiple formats, and digital libraries have made high-quality reading material available to people who would previously have been priced out. I think that is an enormous democratising force, even if it is rarely discussed in those terms. On the other hand, the same devices that give us all this access are also brilliantly engineered to fragment our attention. Reading a long, demanding book on a phone means constantly resisting notifications and the temptation to switch tabs, and I think that has trained a lot of us, myself included, to skim rather than to read deeply. Audiobooks and podcasts have grown enormously, which is wonderful for people who never had time for traditional reading, but I do wonder whether listening engages the brain in quite the same way, especially for complex argument. What I find most interesting, though, is that the definition of reading itself seems to be shifting. For younger readers, reading often blends text, images, video, and discussion threads, which is closer to a multimedia conversation than to the solitary, linear experience that previous generations grew up with. So overall, I would say technology has made reading more accessible and more varied, but also more shallow and more distracted, and the long-term effects on how we think are probably only beginning to show.

key_phrases.phrase: more profound and more subtle than most people realise
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 대부분의 사람들이 깨닫는 것보다 더 깊고 미묘한
